text: The surgeon navigates towards the chest cavity after initial dissection of the stomach, with the diaphragm visible in the upper part of the surgical field.
The surgeon holds the liver aside while grasping the esophagus (gullet) and cuts alongside it, clearing surrounding tissue using laparoscopic instruments.

The surgeon uses a fan retractor to hold back the liver while continuing the dissection to clear blood supply and attachments, preparing to free the esophagus and create an alternative gut from the stomach.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel, an ultrasound-based instrument, to precisely separate tissues surrounding the esophagus.
The surgeon frees the passage of the esophagus, visually identifying the area where the esophagus descends from the chest into the abdomen.
The video shows the pulsation of the aorta, a large artery, visible below the diaphragm as it descends from the heart into the chest cavity.
The surgeon continues the dissection into the mediastinum, the central compartment of the thoracic cavity, carefully separating the esophagus from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon continues the dissection to free the esophagus from surrounding tissues, carefully moving the pulsatile surface of the heart away from the esophagus.
The surgeon continues the dissection to free the esophagus from surrounding tissues, ensuring complete mobilization within the mediastinum.

The surgeon exposes the entire esophagus, or gullet, extending into the mediastinum. Upon reaching the upper end, the dissection continues from the neck to remove the remaining portion of the esophagus.
In the laparoscopic view, the surgeon pulls the mobilized esophagus and part of the stomach from the neck through the gap in the chest, completing the mobilization of the gullet.
The surgeon stitches the artificial tube created from the stomach to the remaining gullet above, completing the thoracoscopic esophagectomy procedure.
